result:
The morning light hit different that Tuesday, slanting through kitchen blinds I'd meant to fix for three years. Coffee gone cold. A dog barking somewhere down the block, rhythmic, insistent, probably chasing something invisible through a chain-link fence. I thought about my mother saying *stillness is a practice* and how I never learned it, how my body hums with the need to be elsewhere even when I'm exactly where I chose to be.

There's a particular shade of blue that exists only in late October, when the sky remembers summer but the trees have already surrendered. I saw it once driving through Ohio, cornfields giving way to sudden hills, and I pulled over because the beauty felt violent, demanded witness. Sat on the hood of a car that smelled like fast food and desperation. Watched a red-tailed kite hover on thermals without moving its wings, held by something I couldn't see.

We don't talk enough about the loneliness of having preferences. How every choice excludes infinite others. I order the same sandwich at the deli not because I love it most but because the weight of deciding once was enough, and now I carry it like a talisman against chaos. The guy behind the counter knows my order. This should comfort me. Sometimes it does. Sometimes I want to scream *surprise me* and see what happens, but I never do.

Memory is a con artist. I can picture my childhood bedroom with impossible clarity—the water stain shaped like Florida on the ceiling, the closet door that wouldn't fully close, the exact pitch of floorboard that squeaked if you stepped wrong coming in past curfew. But last Tuesday? Gone. Dissolved into *work, probably, some emails, dinner, bed.* We're all writing our autobiographies in disappearing ink.

I knew someone once who collected found photographs. Bought them at estate sales, flea markets, shoeboxes of strangers' Christmases and graduations and trips to the Grand Canyon. She'd arrange them on her walls, these faces without stories, and make up elaborate histories for them. *This is Eleanor. She wanted to be a pilot but married a dentist in 1957. She never stopped wanting.* I asked if the fiction ever felt like violence, and she said *the violence is already done, this is just keeping them breathing.*

The body keeps its own calendar. My left knee knows rain twelve hours before it arrives. There's a scar on my palm from a window I broke at seventeen, reaching back into a house I'd locked myself out of, and I still sometimes feel the glass going in, that particular cold-hot of injury, the absurd thought that *my mother will be angry* mixing with *I am bleeding* mixing with *the cat needs to be fed.* The cat was fine. I was fine. We are all so often fine in ways that make no sense.

I want to tell you about the time I heard a nightingale, real ones, not the metaphor kind poets trade like currency. It was accidental, wrong place wrong time right everything, walking through a park in Berlin at 3 AM because I couldn't sleep and the hotel room felt like a coffin for the not-yet-dead. The song came from somewhere I couldn't locate, liquid and ridiculous and completely uninterested in my listening. I stood there until it stopped, and then I stood there longer, waiting for something I couldn't name.

We're losing the names of things. Not the big ones—*tree, river, love, grief*—but the specific, the *black-eyed Susan* becoming *yellow flower,* the *red-tailed hawk* becoming *big bird,* the way we flatten the world to make it manageable and lose the management of wonder. I saw a child point at a monarch butterfly last summer and say *butterfly* with perfect satisfaction, and I felt grief for every migration that child will never need to understand, every impossible journey reduced to a generic flutter.

There's a theory I read somewhere that time doesn't pass, we pass through it, and something about this breaks me open every time I try to hold it. All moments existing simultaneously, our movement the only illusion. My grandmother alive and dying and not-yet-born. This coffee cold and hot and still beans growing on a mountainside I'll never visit. The nightingale singing forever in that Berlin park, me always arriving, always leaving, always standing in the impossible middle.

I write these words and they are already behind me, already becoming something I made rather than something I am making. The cursor blinks. The dog has stopped barking. The light has moved, imperceptibly, toward afternoon, and I have used my thousand words to say *I was here, I noticed, it mattered*—the oldest human spell, the one we never stop casting, the one that never stops mattering, even when no one answers, even when the echo comes back strange, even when we can't be sure we spoke at all.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
